coredata.dc:title: Silver ion-regulated ratiometric fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase detection based on carbon dots and o-phenylenediamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Peihua
Liang, Na
Liu, Chao
Xia, Lian
Qu, Fengli
Song, Zhi-Ling
Kong, Rong-Mei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel silver ion (Ag+)-regulated ratiometric fluorescence method for the effective and sensitive determination of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was established based on carbon dots (CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). OPD can be oxidized by Ag+ to generate fluorescent 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Thus, based on inner-filter effect (IFE) or/and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and DAP, the CDs-Ag+-OPD system can generate dual-emission at 454 nm and 570 nm respectively when excited at 360 nm. The introduction of ascorbic acid (AA) can react with Ag+ to produce dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), which inhibits the generation of DAP, resulting in the fluorescence decrease at 570 nm and fluorescence recovery of CDs at 454 nm. Meanwhile, DHAA can react with OPD to generate quoxaline (QX), which emits strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm, further inhibiting the IFE or/and FRET between CDs and DAP. An obvious ratiometric fluorescence response was observed with the increase of the concentration of AA introduced. Due to the fact that AA can be generated by the enzyme catalysis reaction between ALP and 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid (AAP), the CDs-Ag+-OPD ratiometric system was applied to the determination of ALP successfully. The ratiometric fluorescence value of F 454/F 570 increases with increasing ALP concentration, with a linear range of 0.2 to 40 U/L and detection limit of 0.1 U/L. In addition, the CDs-Ag+-OPD ratiometric system was successfully applied to the detection of ALP in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots
Silver ions
O-phenylenediamine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility research for ratiometric detection of ALP
Detection ALP in human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Education Innovation Program of Qufu Normal University
Qingdao University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Qingdao University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2020KB020 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22074080 ), the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201926), and the Graduate Education Innovation Program of Qufu Normal University (CXJ1903).

The fluorescence detections of ALP were conducted in 500 μL microcentrifuge tubes. First, 10 μL different concentrations of ALP and 10 μL of AAP (6 mM) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min to produce of AA. Then, 40 μL of CDs (100 times diluted), 40 μL of OPD aqueous solution (1 mM), 40 μL of Ag+ aqueous solution (0.5 mM) and 260 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0) was successively added into the above solution and reacted at room temperature for 20 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The practical application of the designed probe was further evaluated by determination of the recoveries of spiked ALP at three different concentrations in the diluted human serum samples. Specifically, the human serum sample was diluted 50 times with PBS buffer, and then tested according to ALP determination procedure. The recovery tests were performed by spiking a certain amount of ALP standard solution (2, 10, 20 U/L) into the diluted human serum samples. Human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers at the Hospital of Qufu Normal University. The related experiments were approved by the Ethical Committee Qufu Normal University, and performed according to its Guidelines.This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020KB020), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074080), the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201926), and the Graduate Education Innovation Program of Qufu Normal University (CXJ1903).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) The size distribution histogram of CDs. (C) The XRD diffraction pattern of CDs. (D) The FT-IR spectra of CDs.

(A) The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. Inset: the photos of CDs solution under natural light and UV lamp. (B) The fluorescence spectra of Ag+-OPD system and the UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD (dotted line) and Ag+-OPD system (solid line). Inset: the photos of OPD and Ag+-OPD system solutions under natural light and UV lamp. (C) The spectral overlap between CD and Ag+-OPD systems. (D) Zeta potentials of CDs, Ag+-OPD and CDs-Ag+-OPD.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs-Ag+-OPD system under different conditions. Inset: the fluorescence photos of (a) CDs + OPD + Ag+, (b) CDs + OPD + Ag+ + AA, (c) CDs + OPD + Ag+ + AAP, (d) CDs + OPD + Ag+ + ALP, (e) CDs + OPD + Ag+ + AAP + ALP. Concentrations: [OPD] = 100 μM, [Ag+] = 50 μM, [AA] = 30 μM, [AAP] = 100 μM, [ALP] = 30 U/L.

(A) The effect of the OPD concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the Ag+-OPD system, [Ag+] = 50 μM. (B) The effect of the dilution times of CDs on the fluorescence responses of CDs-Ag+-OPD system in the absence and presence of AA, [Ag+] = 50 μM, [OPD] = 100 μM, [AA] = 20 μM. The Fold refers to the ratio of F 454/F 570 with and without AA. (C) The effect of the AAP concentration on the fluorescence responses of CDs-Ag+-OPD system for ALP detection, [Ag+] = 50 μM, [OPD] = 100 μM, [CDs] = 1000 diluted times, [ALP] = 20 U/L. (D) The effect of the ALP incubation time on the fluorescence responses of CDs-Ag+-OPD system for ALP detection, [Ag+] = 50 μM, [OPD] = 100 μM, [CDs] = 1000 diluted times, [AAP] = 150 μM, [ALP] = 20 U/L.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system to ALP with different concentrations. Inset: the photo shows the visual fluorescence color change under UV lamp of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system corresponding to the ALP concentrations from a to h are 0, 5, 15, 20, 30, 40, 60 and 80 U/L, respectively. (B) The relationship of F 454/F 570 with various ALP concentrations from 0 − 80 U/L. Inset: The linear relationship of F 454/F 570 with ALP at low concentrations. (C) Absorption spectra of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system to ALP with different concentrations. Inset: the photo shows the visual color change under natural light of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system corresponding to the ALP concentrations from a to h are 0, 5, 15, 20, 30, 40, 60 and 80 U/L, respectively. (D) The relationship of absorbance at 415 nm with various ALP concentrations from 0 − 80 U/L. Inset: The linear relationship of absorbance at 415 nm with ALP at low concentrations.

The selectivity of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system towards ALP against various interferent. Concentrations: [ALP] = 40 U/L, the metal ions are all 10 mM, the amino acids and Hep are all 200 μM, the HSA and BSA are all 0.2 mg/mL, [Trypsine] = 50 U/L, GSH and Cys are all 50 μM, [NEM] = 100 μM.
Schematic illustration of the principle for ratiometric fluorescence detecting of ALP.
Spiked recovery of ALP in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Silver ion-regulated ratiometric fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase detection based on carbon dots and o-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel CDs-Ag+-OPD system was conveniently prepared as ratiometric fluorescent nanoplatform for sensitive and selective detection of ALP.

In this work, a novel silver ion (Ag+)-regulated ratiometric fluorescence method for the effective and sensitive determination of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was established based on carbon dots (CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). OPD can be oxidized by Ag+ to generate fluorescent 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Thus, based on inner-filter effect (IFE) or/and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and DAP, the CDs-Ag+-OPD system can generate dual-emission at 454 nm and 570 nm respectively when excited at 360 nm. The introduction of ascorbic acid (AA) can react with Ag+ to produce dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), which inhibits the generation of DAP, resulting in the fluorescence decrease at 570 nm and fluorescence recovery of CDs at 454 nm. Meanwhile, DHAA can react with OPD to generate quoxaline (QX), which emits strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm, further inhibiting the IFE or/and FRET between CDs and DAP. An obvious ratiometric fluorescence response was observed with the increase of the concentration of AA introduced. Due to the fact that AA can be generated by the enzyme catalysis reaction between ALP and 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid (AAP), the CDs-Ag+-OPD ratiometric system was applied to the determination of ALP successfully. The ratiometric fluorescence value of F 454/F 570 increases with increasing ALP concentration, with a linear range of 0.2 to 40 U/L and detection limit of 0.1 U/L. In addition, the CDs-Ag+-OPD ratiometric system was successfully applied to the detection of ALP in human serum samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data at the Attach File Step.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological enzymes play crucial roles in human bodies which are involved in both human health and disease [1–3], monitoring these enzymes activity is of great importance for understanding their physiological functions and pathological effects in human bodies. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important in phosphate metabolism that can catalyze the dephosphorylation process of proteins, nucleic acids, and small molecules in alkaline media [4]. ALP exists in a wide variety of tissues and plays an important role in signal transmission and regulation of intracellular processes. The abnormality of ALP in human serum is related to some diseases, such as diabetes mellitus, liver disease, Alzheimer’s disease, bone metastasis from prostate carcinoma [5,6]. Therefore, ALP has been extensively studied in clinical practice and widely used as a crucial biomarker for medical diagnostics. As a consequence, the development of facile and effective method for the detection of ALP level is of significance in cases of medical assay and diagnosis.

Up to now, several methods have been developed for the determination of ALP, such as colorimetric [7], fluorescence [8–10], electrochemistry [11], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [12], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13]. Although many of these approaches were well-established, various defects of some methods mentioned above still cannot be ignored that might limit their practical applications. For instance, electrochemical assays usually require laborious steps of washing and assembly of electrodes; SERS assays require expensive and sophisticated instruments; colorimetric assay, although relatively simple, usually suffers from inadequate sensitivity. Among them, fluorescence method has attracted a great deal of interest due to its offering of more advantages over others, such as simple operation, high sensitivity, good reproducibility and can be easily applied in imaging in vitro and in vivo [14–17]. Several efficient indicators such as organic fluorescence dyes have been deployed for the fluorescence determination of ALP [10,18–19]. In the past decade, various kinds of fluorescence nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs) [20], coordination polymer nanoparticles [21], polydopamine nanoparticles [8], and metal nanoclusters [4,22,23], turn out to be more useful for the design of fluorescent probes for the determination of ALP. Despite these systems displayed good performances, the fluorescence signal output based on the change of single fluorescent emission mode. In this way, the incident light, probe concentration, as well as environmental effects may interfere with the quantitative detection that result in false positive responses and less sensitive consequences. It is well known that the ratiometric fluorescence method employing two or more independent emission bands can eliminate most of the interferents effectively [24–26]. In recent years, researchers have been working on ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of ALP and have achieved remarkable development [27–30]. However, many probes have disadvantages such as complex synthesis or operation and low water solubility or biocompatibility, so it is still necessary to develop simpler and more convenient ratiometric fluorescent probes to determine ALP.

As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit fascinating benefits, such as being adjustable emission wavelength, strong light stability, good biocompatible and simple preparation, making them ideal options for biosensing, bioimaging and biomedicine [15,31–33]. It is well known that the colorless o-phenlyenediamine (OPD) can be oxidized into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with strong fluorescence emission [34,35]. Among them, the metal ions Ag+, as a simple and readily available oxidant, has attracted wide attention and interest in the oxidation of OPD [36,37]. However, the ascorbic acid (AA) that exhibits high reducing capabilities could inhibit the generation of fluorescent DAP via the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 [38,39]. In addition, the condensation reaction of the resulting product of dehydro-ascorbic acid (DHAA) with OPD to form quinoxaline (QX) with strong blue fluorescence is usually applied for the fluorescence determination of AA [38,40]. Therefore, the inhibition of Ag+ oxidation ability by AA and the consumption of OPD via the production of QX can synergistically decrease the generation of fluorescent DAP. As most CDs are negatively charged while OPD is positively charged in general physiological environmental conditions, OPD can be adsorbed onto the surface of CDs by electrostatic interaction. The π-π stacking between CDs and OPD molecules is also one of the reasons that OPD can be adsorbed onto CDs surface. In this way, if there is spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of CDs and the excitation or absorption spectrum of DAP, the inner-filter effect (IFE) or/and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) could occur between them with CDs acted as the donor and DAP as the acceptor [26].

Herein, inspired by these desirable facts, we proposed a novel Ag+-regulated convenient ratiometric fluorescence assay for sensitive and selective detection of ALP based on CDs and OPD (Scheme 1 ). When Ag+ is introduced into the mixed system of CDs and OPD, a dual-emission phenomenon occurs through IFE or/and FRET between CDs and DAP which is generated from the oxidation of OPD by Ag+. However, after ALP hydrolyzes AAP to produce AA, the oxidation capacity of Ag+ to OPD will be inhibited. Meanwhile, DHAA can quickly react with OPD produce blue fluorescent QX, further reducing the generation of DAP, resulting in a ratiometric response with CDs fluorescence recovery and DAP fluorescence decline. Therefore, the label-free fluorescent CDs-Ag+-OPD system can be used to ratiometric detecting of ALP with the advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity. Meanwhile, with naked eyes, it can be clearly observed the color change of the final system gradually turns from pale yellow to colorless under visible light and the intense yellow fluorescence gradually turns to pale bule under ultraviolet with the increasing concentration of ALP. Moreover, the method successfully applied for the detection of ALP in human serum samples.

Ascorbic acid (AA), 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid (AAP), dopamine (DA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), tyrosine (Tyr), glutamic acid (Glu), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Try), alanine (Ala), threonine (Thr), glutathione (GSH), Cys, heparin, trypsine, melamine and sodium citrate were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). AgNO3 and other inorganic salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co. ltd. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) Co. ltd. Human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), reduced N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The buffer solution used in the experiment was 10 mM NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 (PBS, pH 8.0). All of the reagents were analytical grade without more purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm) produced by Millipore system.

All the fluorescent spectra were performed by F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with excitation wavelength at 360 nm using 10 nm/10 nm slit width and PMT detector voltage 700 V. UV–vis spectra were recorded by UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Morphological characterization of CDs was carried out by JEM-2100PLUS transmission electron microscope (TEM, Japan). Fourier transform near-infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer was used for recording the infrared spectra (Thermo Fisher, USA). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern Spectrum was carried out on a MiniFlex600 X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Zeta potential was measured at room temperature using Malvern Zetasizer Nano Instrument (Malvern Instruments ltd., UK). The photos were taken by WFH-203B box-like ultraviolet analyzer (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were prepared according to the previously reported procedure [41]. Briefly, 0.24 g of melamine and 0.58 g of sodium citrate were dissolved in 25 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 5 min, and then transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor for the hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. After that, the cooled reaction solution was filtered with a cylindrical membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove large dots. The prepared CDs were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The fluorescence detections of ALP were conducted in 500 μL microcentrifuge tubes. First, 10 μL different concentrations of ALP and 10 μL of AAP (6 mM) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min to produce of AA. Then, 40 μL of CDs (100 times diluted), 40 μL of OPD aqueous solution (1 mM), 40 μL of Ag+ aqueous solution (0.5 mM) and 260 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0) was successively added into the above solution and reacted at room temperature for 20 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The practical application of the designed probe was further evaluated by determination of the recoveries of spiked ALP at three different concentrations in the diluted human serum samples. Specifically, the human serum sample was diluted 50 times with PBS buffer, and then tested according to ALP determination procedure. The recovery tests were performed by spiking a certain amount of ALP standard solution (2, 10, 20 U/L) into the diluted human serum samples. Human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers at the Hospital of Qufu Normal University. The related experiments were approved by the Ethical Committee Qufu Normal University, and performed according to its Guidelines.

The morphology and structure of CDs were characterized by TEM, XRD and FT-IR to confirm their successful synthesis. As the TEM image shown in Fig. 1 A, the prepared CDs have good dispersion and uniform size distribution. The particle size distribution is in the range of 3–6 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 4.7 nm (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (the inset in Fig. 1A) shows the lattice spacing of CD particle is 0.25 nm, which corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite, indicating the graphite-like structure. The XRD profile shows a broad diffraction peak at (2θ) = 27°, which is attributed to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating the consistence with the HRTEM characterization (Fig. 1C). The surface composition of the CDs was measured by FT-IR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1D, the wide peak at 3421 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, which indicates the existence of amine and hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1577 cm−1 associates with the stretching vibrations of CO and CC. The peaks at 1401 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 belong to the CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. These characterization results are consistent with the previously reported CDs [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility research for ratiometric detection of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: The principle for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ALP is shown in Scheme 1. Based on IFE or/and FRET between CDs and DAP, single excitation and dual-emission can be obtained. The presence of AA can inhibit the generation of DAP, and DHAA, which is the oxidation product of AA, can further consume OPD, resulting in a synergistic effect on the inhibition of IFE or/and FRET, realizing the ratiometric response detection of AA. ALP can catalyze AAP to produce AA, therefore, the CDs-Ag+-OPD system can be further used for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ALP. First, a series of spectroscopic investigations were carried out to verify the feasibility of the ratiometic response of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system for the detection of ALP. The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were employed to investigate the optical performance of the CDs and Ag+-OPD system. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs exhibits a strong absorption peak at 330 nm, which is mainly produced by the electronic transition of aromatic conjugated systems CC and CO bonds of CDs [41]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs exhibit that the maximum excitation and emission peaks are at 360 nm and at 460 nm, respectively. Fig. 2B shows that the OPD is easily oxidized to DAP by Ag+, which can emit strong yellow fluorescence at 570 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 420 nm. Simultaneously, a color change of the solution from colorless to light yellow can be observed, with an obvious appearance of the characteristic absorption peak for DAP at about 430 nm. Since the excitation and absorption spectra of DAP and the emission spectrum of CDs overlapped at the wavelength range of 400–480 nm (Fig. 2C), the CDs and Ag+-OPD could be used for the design of a ratiometric fluorescent probe via the IFE or/and FRET mechanism. In addition, as the covering of –COOH and –OH groups of the CDs surface with easy donate proton ability, the CDs surface are negatively charged and assessed by the zeta potential measurement. Fig. 2D shows that the zeta potential of CDs is −10.6 mV. However, when Ag+-OPD is added into CDs solution, the zeta potential of CDs-Ag+-OPD increases obviously to −6.28 mV, indicating the existence of electrostatic adsorption between CDs and DAP and providing the possibility for FRET generation between CDs and DAP.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the emission spectrum of CDs-Ag+-OPD system verified the above inference, appearing the dual-emission phenomenon with the CDs fluorescence decrease and the DAP fluorescence increase under the excitation at 360 nm. AA is a common reducing agent for the reduction of Ag+ into Ag0 while AA can be oxidized to DHAA [42]. Therefore, the introduction of AA into CDs-Ag+-OPD system could inhibit the generation of DAP, resulting in the fluorescence increasing of CDs and the fluorescence decrease of DAP. Then, the produced DHAA can react with OPD quickly to generate QX which has strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm with the best excitation wavelength at 360 nm (Fig. S1). In this way, the synergistic effect of AA on DAP production and DHAA on OPD consumption leads to more obvious ratiometric changes between blue fluorescence and yellow fluorescence. As one of the hydrolysis substrates of ALP, AAP can be hydrolyzed to produce AA, so the CDs-Ag+-OPD system can be further used for the detection of ALP. Fig. 3 shows that the presence of AAP or ALP alone has little effect on the fluorescence of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system, while the concurrence of ALP and AAP induced significant ratiometric fluorescence change, indicating the feasibility for ALP detection. As expected, the absorption of the solutions also changed correspondingly (Fig. S2).

Since the oxidation of OPD depends on the regulation of Ag+, while in essence, the ratiometric signal changes of CDs-Ag+-OPD system for ALP detection is caused by the reduction of Ag+ by AA. In that case, the concentration of Ag+ should not be too high. Therefore, the concentration of Ag+ was fixed at 50 μM to investigate the influence of OPD concentration on the fluorescence of Ag+-OPD system. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of OPD concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm of the Ag+-OPD system increased obviously, indicating the increasing amount of DAP produced. When the OPD concentration increased to 100 μM, the fluorescence increase slowed down, which was related to the 1:2 quantitative reaction between the Ag+ and OPD. In order to achieve a good inhibition effect of AA on DAP generation, 100 μM was used as the optimal concentration of OPD. In order to obtain satisfactory IFE or/and FRET performance, the effect of CDs dilution ratio on the dual-emission fluorescence of CDs-Ag+-OPD system was then investigated. Fig. 4B shows that the CDs-Ag+-OPD system composed of CDs diluted 1000 times (final concentration) can obtain the best dual-emission fluorescence property. As the enzymatic substrate for ALP, the concentration of AAP was also investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 4C, with the gradual increase of AAP concentration until it reaches 125 μM, the production of AA gradually increases due to the enzyme-catalyzed hydrolysis reaction of ALP, resulting in the increase of the F 454/F 570 value. In order to ensure complete enzymatic reaction, 150 μM was selected as the optimal AAP concentration. In addition, the effect of ALP incubation time on fluorescence response was investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 4D, the F 454/F 570 reached the maximum value when the ALP incubation time was 20 min. In addition, the effect of pH on the fluorescence responses of the F 454/F 570 value was studied (Fig. S3). The experimental result shows that the probe has the largest fluorescence response fold at pH 8.0 for the detection of ALP, thus pH 8.0 was selected as the optimal pH for the enzymatic reaction.

The fluorescence and absorbance responses of CDs-Ag+-OPD system towards different concentration of ALP were investigated under optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity decreases at 570 nm while increases at 454 nm gradually with the increase of ALP concentration. This is because the AA generated by the hydrolysis of AAP by ALP inhibits the production of Ag+ regulated DAP, thereby blocking the IFE or/and FRET between CDs and DAP. At the same time, the synergistic effect of the generation of blue emitting QX further consumes OPD, resulting in obvious ratiometric fluorescence responses with the increase of ALP concentration. The inset image clearly shows that the fluorescence color gradually changed from bright yellow to blue with the ALP concentration increased under UV lamp. Fig. 5B shows that the ratiometric intensity of F 454/F 570 gradually increases with increasing of ALP concentration. The ratiometric intensity of F 454/F 570 shows a good linear relationship with the concentration of ALP in the range of 0.2–40 U/L. The linear regression equation is F 454/F 570 = 0.5417 + 0.0454[ALP] (R2 = 0.995), and a detection limit of 0.1 U/L can be estimated based on 3σ/slope rule. The analytical performance of this proposed CDs-Ag+-OPD system was compared with those previous reported fluorescent assays for ALP. As shown in Table S1, comparable/superior detection limit and linear range for ALP can be observed owing to the ratiometric fluorescence response. The UV–vis absorption investigation indicated that the absorbance at 415 nm gradually decreased with the increasing of ALP concentration, while high concentration of ALP resulted in the appearance of new absorption peak at 345 nm, indicating the generation of QX (Fig. 5C). A linear absorbance relationship with the concentration of ALP in the range of 0.2–40 U/L can be obtained which is consistent with the linear range of fluorescence detection. The linear regression equation is A 415 = 0.4000–0.0062 [ALP] (R2 = 0.993), and a detection limit of 0.2 U/L can be estimated based on 3σ/slope rule. Moreover, the solutions exhibit obvious color change from light yellow to colorless. The distinctive color change of the above fluorescence and absorbance investigation that can be observed with the naked eye makes this ratiometric sensing system simpler and more convenient for the detection of ALP.

Some metal ions, amino acids, proteins, and oxidation-reducing substances etc. are common potential interferent in human physiological sample which may lead to the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence system, thus affecting the specificity towards ALP detection. Therefore, the responses of the CDs-Ag+-OPD system to these potential interfering species under the same experimental conditions were investigated to explore the selectivity for ALP detection. Fig. 6 shows the distinctions of ratiometric fluorescence value of F 454/F 570 for these interfering substances with ALP. It could be observed that except GSH and Cys, only ALP could trigger significant changes of F 454/F 570 in this way. Other interferent had negligible influences on the proposed system. The obvious F 454/F 570 changes caused by GSH and Cys are due to their similar reducibility to AA. Fortunately, when NEM is added, a scavenger that reacts specifically with sulfhydryl compounds can effectively eliminate the interference of GSH and Cys, while NEM itself does not interfere with the system fluorescence. In addition, the mixtures of ALP with every interferent also exhibited satisfactory ratiometric fluorescence responses (Fig. S4). These results indicated that the proposed sensing system exhibit high selectivity for ALP detection, which provides the possibility to apply the system to the detection of ALP in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection ALP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of the proposed method for practical applications was investigated in the human serum samples. Three human serum samples were taken from healthy adult volunteers. Given the normal level of ALP in adult human serum (40–150 U/L) [4,43] and the linear range of the proposed method, the serum samples were diluted 50-fold before tested not only to ensure the ALP concentration stayed within the linear range but also reduce the concentration of interfering substances. The ALP levels found in the three serum samples were consistent with hospital reports. Then, the recoveries of spiked different concentration of ALP in diluted human serums were carried out. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries are range from 97.2 % to 104.9 % with the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 4.7 %. These results suggested that the CDs-Ag+-OPD sensing system has great potential to be applied to ALP detection in clinical diagnosis.

In summary, we have developed a facile and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence method for detection of ALP based on the regulating the oxidation capacity of Ag+ to OPD and combining with CDs. By integrating CDs and OPD into one sensing system, a strongly fluorescent DAP was generated by the Ag+ oxidation of OPD, which formed dual-emission with CDs based on IFE or/and FRET. When ALP hydrolyzes AAP to produce AA, it inhibits the oxidation of OPD by Ag+ and reduces the yield of DAP. Moreover, the generated DHAA could further react with OPD to produce blue fluorescent QX, which synergistically reduces yellow fluorescence emission and enhances blue fluorescence emission, resulting in a proportional fluorescence response. The integration of CDs and OPD into one sensing system not only shows the advantages of simplicity, cost-effectivity and lowly laborious operation but also realizes high sensitivity and selectivity for ALP detection. In addition, this method is successfully applied to the determination of ALP in human serum samples, manifesting its feasibility in the real sample detection and further potential in monitoring of the disease biomarkers in the clinical medicine.

Peihua Li: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Na Liang: Data curation. Lian Xia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fengli Qu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Zhi-Ling Song: Writing – review & editing. Rong-Mei Kong: Methodology, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020KB020), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074080), the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201926), and the Graduate Education Innovation Program of Qufu Normal University (CXJ1903).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121682.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
